segments.s447-g17: This is a vocative conditional clause: 'تو تا باشی' literally 'you until be' = 'so long as you remain'. 'تو' is the 2nd-person singular subject, 'تا' marks the temporal limit ('as long as' / 'until'), and 'باشی' is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'you remain' rather than an imperative here. 'ای خسرو پاک و راد' is a vocative address ('O king, pure and generous') following the clause; Persian often places the vocative after the temporal phrase.
segments.s447-g18: 'مرنجان' is an imperative negative (second-person) meaning 'do not vex / do not make upset' and governs the rest of the clause. 'کسی را که دارد نژاد' is the object clause: 'کسی' (someone), 'را' marks it as a definite/affected object, and the relative 'که دارد نژاد' ('who has lineage') specifies which people are meant. Word order is object + relative clause after the verb of the main clause, a common Persian pattern.
segments.s447-g19: 'چو' here means 'when' and introduces a temporal clause. 'فرزند سام نریمان' is a genitive/appositive phrase: 'Nariman, son (lit. child/descendant) of Sam' — Persian allows this name + patronymic sequence without an explicit 'of'. 'ز بند' uses the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from' and 'بند' = 'fetter / bond', giving 'from his bonds'.
segments.s447-g20: 'بنالد' is a past/episodic narrative verb meaning 'he lamented / moaned' (3rd person). It takes the prepositional complement 'به پروردگار بلند' — 'to/unto the lofty Lord' — where 'به' marks the indirect object/recipient and 'بلند' is an epithet of the Lord ('lofty'). In epic Persian, short word order (verb then object/compliment) is common for vivid narration.
segments.s447-g21: 'بپیچی' is a subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن meaning 'you/one may turn' or 'one turns aside'; here it expresses a hypothetical or concessive action introduced by 'گرچه' ('although/even if'). 'ازان' = 'from that/it'. 'گرچه' signals the concession, and 'نیک‌اختری' functions as a qualifying phrase ('a favorable/fortunate star' or 'one favored by a good star'); the phrase as a whole means 'even if (one) turns away from that, even if (favored) by a good star.'
segments.s447-g22: 'چو' again means 'when' here. 'با' is instrumental/agentive 'by' (i.e., 'by the Creator'), 'کردگار' is 'the Maker/Creator (God)', and 'افگند' is a past simple verb meaning 'cast/let fall'. 'داوری' is the object 'judgment'. Together: 'when by the Creator judgment is cast (down)'. This clause presents the inevitable divine action despite previous concessions.
segments.s447-g23: 'چو' = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. 'رستم نگهدار تخت کیان' is a title-like apposition: 'Rostam, the keeper/guardian of the throne of the Kians' (تخت کیان = 'throne of the Kayanians/royal house'). Persian frequently stacks name + title + possessive noun in this compact way.
segments.s447-g24: 'همی' is a habitual/continuous marker ('used to / continually'), and 'بر در' means 'at/on the gate' with 'در' here meaning 'gate/door'. 'رنج' = 'suffering/hardship' and 'بستی' (perfect/habitual past) = 'you/ he shut/blocked' (here 'barred'). 'میان' functions as 'middle' = 'in the midst of' or 'amid'. Together: 'he would continually bar the gate amid hardship.'
segments.s447-g57: This is the reporting clause with the indirect object first (به بهمن = 'to Bahman'), then the adverbial manner چنین گفت ('thus said'). کای is a vocative particle 'O', followed by شاه نو 'new king' as the addressee. Note the archaic/poetic ordering: the object/recipient precedes the verb گفت (past 'said').
segments.s447-g58: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause here meaning 'when'. بر + نیمه آسمان is a spatial phrase 'on the mid‑sky' and ماه نو 'new moon' is the subject/locative referent. Word order is typical poetic Persian: temporal 'چو' + prepositional phrase + noun phrase (ماه نو).
segments.s447-g59: This is an imperative instruction with the temporal/adverbial modifier 'به شبگیر' ('at nightfall' / 'by night'). ازین مرز ('from this frontier') marks origin; لشکر بران is the verbal phrase 'send/dispatch the army' in poetic diction (بران is an archaic/imperative form). The verb is deferred to the end, a common poetic order.
segments.s447-g60: که here gives the reason/explanation 'for/because'. این کار دشوار گشت is a past/result clause 'this work/undertaking became difficult' (گشت = became). و گران adds a second predicate/adjective 'and burdensome/heavy', linked by the copulative و.
segments.s447-g61: This is an optative/blessing clause introduced elliptically: ز تاج تو ('from/of your crown') gives the reference, then چشم بدان دور باد is a formulaic wish where باد is the optative particle 'may' and دور 'distant'. بدان here functions as a fused prepositional phrase ('به آن' = 'to/from that') in poetic spelling. Word order places the locative/possessor first for emphasis.
segments.s447-g62: Another optative sentence: همه روزگاران تو ('all your days/ages') is the subject phrase and سور باد is the wish 'may it be a feast' (سور = feast; باد = 'may'). The order is subject followed by the optative predicate, typical for blessings.
segments.s447-g63: بدین خانه is a locative/introductory phrase 'in/at this house' (بدین = 'by/with/at this' in poetic usage). The names زال سام are listed as occupants/figures and دلیر is an epithet 'the brave' attached to سام. Word order places the locative phrase first for topical emphasis.
segments.s447-g64: This clause contains a conditional/optative formula: سزد‌گر is a concessive/conditional 'if it befit' or 'one who deems fit' used here as 'if it seems proper'; نماند is a present/future-oriented negative 'will not remain / does not remain' (poetic presentive sense), شهنشاه 'the emperor' is the subject, and دیر 'long' modifies duration. The clause thus states that, if fitting, the sovereign will not stay long.
segments.s447-g1: گرامی پشوتن is an epithet + proper name: the adjective گرامی ('honored/noble') precedes the name پشوتن. که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') and دستور is the predicate noun in that clause; بود is the past copula 'was' placed after the predicate (typical older/poetic order).
segments.s447-g2: ز کشتن is a prepositional phrase 'from/on account of the killing'; دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش). سخت رنجور بود is the predicate: سخت (intensifier/adverb) + رنجور 'distressed/afflicted' and بود 'was' as the past copula, meaning 'was severely distressed.'
segments.s447-g3: به پیش جهاندار is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the sovereign' (به + پیش = 'in front of'). بر پای خاست is an idiomatic verb phrase 'rose to his feet' where بر + پای ('on the foot') is followed by the past verb خاست 'arose.'
segments.s447-g4: چنین گفت is 'thus he spoke/said' (چنین = 'thus'); what follows is a vocative and epithets: کای is the vocative 'O', خسرو the name, and داد و راست are nominal epithets ('justice' and 'uprightness' used to praise him). Note the sequence: narration ('thus said') followed by the words addressed.
segments.s447-g5: اگر introduces a conditional. کینه بودت is an older-form past copular phrase meaning 'if a grudge had been to you' (i.e., 'if you had a grudge'); به دل is the locative 'in the heart'; خواستی is a past-tense/desiderative form 'you wished/would have wished' giving the hypothetical result—together this is a counterfactual protasis.
segments.s447-g6: پدید آمد is a compound verb 'arose/appeared' (پدید 'appeared' + آمد 'came'). از marks the source 'from' and کاستی راستی is a genitival phrase 'a deficiency/lack of righteousness'—literal: 'it arose from a deficiency of rightness.'
segments.s447-g7: This is a paratactic list introduced by کنون 'now': a sequence of nouns (غارت، کشتن، جنگ، جوش) naming violent actions or states; they function as the nominal objects or imperatives' content to follow.
segments.s447-g8: مفرمای is a polite/imperative form 'do/carry out' addressed to the addressee; و links to the next imperative مپسند 'do not approve/do not tolerate.' چندین خروش is the object 'many cries/outcries' (چندین 'many a/several' + خروش 'uproar'), i.e., 'do it, and do not allow many cries.'
segments.s447-g65: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شد is a past tense verb here meaning 'happened/became' (used narratively). The subject کوه (mountain) follows the verb in this poetic word order. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and گونه here is the physical meaning 'cheek' (a common poetic image). سندروس is the noun modified by بر گونه (’on the cheek of sandalwood’); the whole phrase is a locational/figurative phrase describing where/ how the mountain appeared.
segments.s447-g66: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. برخاست is the simple past 'rose/issued forth' (3rd person sing.). آوای is the noun 'the sound/voice' with the -ی/construct sense linking it to the following noun; کوس names the instrument 'war‑drum'. The verb برخاست governs the noun phrase 'آوای کوس' (i.e. 'the drum's sound rose').
segments.s447-g67: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense narrative verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. پس here is adverbial 'then/after that'. بهمن is a proper name (Bahman) used as the addressee/agent of the command. کینه‌خواه is an epithet 'the Avenger' (a compound noun) describing Bahman.
segments.s447-g68: کزانجا is a compound adverb 'from there/thence' marking origin. برانند is the 3rd-person-plural subjunctive/jussive form of راندن used to report an order — 'that they drive/dispatch'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/altogether' modifying سپاه; سپاه is the direct object 'army/host'. Together the phrase expresses the ordered action: 'that from there they send/drive the whole host.'
segments.s447-g69: هم‌انگه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment/then'. برآمد is the past verb 'rose/issued forth'. ز is the preposition 'from'. پرده‌سرای (lit. 'curtain-house') is the place 'pavilion/scene/stage' and here names the source from which the sound issues; the prepositional phrase z + place precedes the verb for emphasis.
segments.s447-g70: This is a nominal string describing the sound and instruments. بوق 'horn/trumpet' is the head noun; و 'and' links بوق to هندی (’Indian’), while درای is an adjective/participle-like word meaning 'having/possessing' that attributes the listed instruments or qualities. In context the phrase names the instruments (horns, Indian instruments) whose sound issues from the pavilion; the precise lexical identities here are poetic/instrumental terms.
segments.s447-g71: از آنجا is the prepositional phrase 'from there'. به introduces the destination 'toward/into' and is followed by ایران (Iran). نهادند is the past 3rd-person-plural of نهادن 'to place/set' meaning here 'they set/turned' (i.e. set their direction). روی is the noun 'face/direction' used idiomatically with نهادند to mean 'they turned their faces toward' (i.e. they headed toward Iran).
segments.s447-g72: به گفتار is a prepositional phrase 'by the word/at the command of' (به + گفتار). دستور is the governing noun 'order/command'. آزاده‌خوی is an epithet 'free‑spirited/magnanimous' attached to دستور (genitive relation), so the whole string means 'by the command/word of the magnanimous (one)'. Grammatically it modifies or gives the authority for the preceding action.
segments.s447-g33: چو here introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when' and precedes the verb. بشنید is a simple past (he heard) with subject شاه (the king) following the verb in the classical / poetic word order (verb before subject). از + اسم (از پشوتن سخن) marks the source/owner of the speech: 'speech from Peshotan' (i.e. Peshotan's words).
segments.s447-g34: پشیمان شد is a two-word predicate: پشیمان (regretful) + شد (became) — شد is the past form of شدن meaning 'became'. از here gives the causal sense 'because of'. درد و کین کهن is a coordinated noun phrase 'pain and (old) enmity/grudge', where کهن qualifies کین as 'ancient/long-standing'.
segments.s447-g35: خروشی برآمد is a nominal+verb pair meaning 'a clamor/roar arose' (برآمد = 'arose/issued forth'). ز introduces the place/source 'from' and پرده‌سرای is the locative/source noun (poetic for the area behind the curtain / stage/curtain-chamber).
segments.s447-g36: که‌ای here introduces the content of the clamor as an address; it functions like an exclamatory/introducing particle followed by the vocative پهلوانان ('O champions'). با داد و رای is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with justice and counsel' (با = 'with', داد = 'justice', رای = 'counsel/plan'), giving the qualities urged on the champions.
segments.s447-g37: بسیچیدن is an imperative-like infinitival/verb stem meaning 'gather/assemble abundantly' (classical poetic verb form). بازگشتن کنید uses بازگشتن (to return) plus کنید (second-person plural imperative/polite exhortation 'do/you do'), so the sense is 'go back / return (you should)'. The verb comes after the verbal noun in this poetic order.
segments.s447-g38: مبادا ... که introduces a prohibitive warning 'lest / may it not be that...'. تاراج و کشتن are the actions warned against; each is followed by کنید (do) so the clause literally says 'lest you do pillaging and killing' — i.e. 'beware lest you plunder and slay'.
segments.s447-g39: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past-tense verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا here has a purposive/so-that sense ('so that' / 'that'), introducing the content: پای دستان ز بند — a genitive/partitive construction where پای (feet) + دستان (of the hands / hands) and ز بند 'from the fetter(s)'. The overall structure expresses the command to loosen the fetters.
segments.s447-g40: گشادند is a past/plural verbal form meaning 'they were loosened/opened' (here passive/auspicious result). و links the next clause: دادند بسیار پند — دادند (they gave) plus بسیار (many) and پند (advice/counsel) yields 'they gave much advice'. Note the verb دادند is active 'they gave'.
segments.s447-g25: The clause has a classical Persian word order with the verb at the end: تو (subject pronoun, emphatic) ... یافتی (past, 2nd person singular). ازو is the older form of از او meaning 'from him', and یادگار follows the verb as an object/complement meaning 'as a keepsake' or 'as a memento.' یافتی is the past-tense perfective 'you obtained/received.'
segments.s447-g26: نه is the negation 'not' applying to the following prepositional phrase. از راه is a prepositional expression 'by/through the way (or line) of'; راه here means 'way/method/lineage.' The proper names are linked by و 'and.' The phrase denies that the crown came via Gushtasp and Esfandiar.
segments.s447-g27: ز هنگامه is a temporal prepositional phrase 'from the moment/occasion.' کی here functions as the temporal relative 'when' (classical usage: 'at the time that'), and قباد is the name; اندرآی is an archaic/persianized present/past-in-context verb meaning 'enter(s)/came in' (here used as 'came in/appeared').
segments.s447-g28: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' تا is the temporal conjunction 'until' linking this span to the following name. به + کیخسرو marks the endpoint ('up to/through Kay Khosrow'), and پاک‌رای is an epithet attached to Kay Khosrow meaning 'of pure repute/virtuous.'
segments.s447-g29: بزرگی is the noun 'greatness' or 'glory' placed before the verb for emphasis. به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'by means of the sword' (به here marks the instrument: 'by/through'), او is the possessor/agent 'his,' and داشتند is the past plural 'they had/possessed.'
segments.s447-g30: مهان را uses the object/definite marker را after مهان 'the guests' (or visitors). همه is the subject 'all/everyone,' زیر او داشتند is a predicate meaning 'were under him' or 'were subject to him' (زیر meaning 'under/subject to'). The verb داشتند is again past plural 'they had/held.'
segments.s447-g31: This is a conditional/actional phrase: ازو 'from him' (of him), بند 'the bond/fetter' (object), بردار‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional or agentive form meaning 'if you remove' or 'one who removes' (here read as the conditional 'if you remove'), and بخردی 'by wisdom/if you are wise' qualifies the action — together: 'if by wisdom you can remove the bond from him.'
segments.s447-g32: دلت is دل + second-person suffix ت 'your heart.' بازگردان is the imperative/causative verb 'turn back/restore' in the 2nd-person form addressing the addressee. ز راه بدی is a prepositional phrase 'from the evil way/path' (ز = 'from', راه = 'path/way', بدی = 'evil/wickedness'), completing the command: 'turn your heart away from the bad path.'
segments.s447-g49: کنون is an adverbial fronting meaning “now.” The noun phrase گنج تاراج is a poetic juxtaposition (noun + noun) meaning “treasure (that has been) plundered” (the modifying relation is implied rather than marked by ezāfe). The conjunction و links the following verbless phrase دستان اسیر (“hands captive”) — Persian poetry often omits the copula/auxiliary, so these phrases describe states/result of the raid.
segments.s447-g50: پسر زار is a noun modified by the adjective زار (“wretched”); کشته is a past participle used predicatively so the clause reads “the wretched son (was) slain.” The preposition به + instrument (به پیکان تیر) marks the instrument/agent (“by an arrow”); the construction omits an explicit auxiliary verb (e.g. شد) because the participle carries the passive meaning.
segments.s447-g51: مبیناد is an archaic/polite 3rd-person verbal form used here with an optative/negative sense—functioning like “may (it) not be seen / may no one behold.” The phrase چشم کس is an inverted possessive/indefinite expression (“anyone’s eye / no eye”); این روزگار is the object/time being referred to (“this age/these times”). Word order is somewhat flexible and elliptical as is frequent in epic verse.
segments.s447-g52: زمین باد uses باد as an optative particle following the noun: “باد” = “may (it be)”, so “زمین باد …” = “may the earth be …”. بی‌تخم is a prefixed adjective meaning “without seed/sterile,” and اسفندیار names whose seed/offspring is being wished away; the adjective phrase follows the verb-like optative, producing a wish: “may the earth be without Esfandiyar’s seed.”
segments.s447-g53: ازان آگهی is an adverbial genitive phrase meaning “from that news / on that information.” سوی بهمن رسید is a light clause in which سوی + proper name indicates the direction/target (“toward Bahman”) and رسید is the past intransitive “reached / arrived” meaning “word/information reached Bahman.” The subject (the news/word) is implied.
segments.s447-g54: به نزدیک is a locative phrase meaning “to/near (the) vicinity,” followed by the person phrase فرخ پشوتن. Here فرخ functions as an epithet/adjective attached to the name پشوتن (literally “fortunate Peshotan”). رسید repeats the verb “arrived / reached,” emphasizing that the same news came to him as well.
segments.s447-g55: پشوتن is the subject (proper name). The preposition ز (= از) marks cause “because of / from” رودابه, and پردرد is an adjective meaning “full of pain/sorrow.” The verb شد (‘became’) follows the adjective to form the resultative “became sorrowful.” This is a standard Persian causative/result expression (subject + ز + cause + predicate + شد).
segments.s447-g56: ازان شیون is an adverbial phrase “from that wailing / because of that lament,” where ازان (= از آن) links cause and result. او رخش is a possessive juxtaposition (“his steed”) typical in older/plain word order (رخش او would be the analytic alternative). زرد شد is a compound of adjective + شد meaning “grew yellow/pale.” The whole clause: “from that wailing his steed grew pale.”
segments.s447-g73: سپه را ز زابل به ایران کشید is a past action with an implicit subject ('he'). سپه + را marks the army as a definite direct object; را is the Persian definite-object marker. ز (poetic for از) indicates origin ('from Zabul'), به ایران shows the goal ('into/to Iran'), and کشید is the past tense of کشیدن used here to mean 'moved/led/caused to go' rather than its agricultural senses.
segments.s447-g74: به نزدیک شهر دلیران کشید is an adverbial/locative continuation: به نزدیک = 'to the vicinity of', شهر دلیران = 'the city of the brave' (the possessive/ezāfe is omitted poetically), and کشید repeats the past verb for stylistic parallelism ('brought/led [it]').
segments.s447-g75: برآسود is a single past-form verb meaning 'he was at rest / ceased to toil' (poetic/compound form). و links clauses, and بر تخت بنشست is the next past clause: بر + تخت = 'upon the throne' and بنشست = 'he sat down'. شاد follows as a postverbal predicate/adjective meaning 'joyful'.
segments.s447-g76: جهان را همی داشت is a habitual/progressive past: همی + داشت gives 'he was holding/ruling' (continuous or customary action); جهان را is the definite object ('the world'). با رسم و داد is an instrumental phrase 'with custom/established practice and justice' describing how he governed. Note همی is an older particle indicating continuous or habitual aspect.
segments.s447-g77: به درویش is the indirect object placed before the verb ('to/for a dervish'). بخشید is the past tense 'he gave' or 'bestowed.' چندی درم is the quantified direct object 'some dirhams' (چندی preceding the noun as a quantifier/indefinite amount).
segments.s447-g78: ازو (from/of him/it) refers back to that gift; the clause omits a copula/verb and uses predicates directly: چند شادان و چندی دژم = 'some (were) joyful and some (were) sullen.' چند vs. چندی is a stylistic variation both meaning 'some/several.' شادان and دژم are predicate adjectives describing recipients or effects.
segments.s447-g79: جهانا is a vocative 'O world' (addressing the world). چه خواهی is a direct interrogative in the second person ('what do you want' / 'what will you have'), with خواهی as present/future 'you want/will want.' ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from,' and پروردگان is the plural noun 'the nurturers/lords/rulers' — here 'rulers' or 'those who raise/possess power.'
segments.s447-g80: چه at the start is exclamative/pejorative 'what (sort of)...' followed by پروردگان (rulers). داغ دل بردگان is a genitive chain 'the dāgh (burning grief/wound) of the heart of the slaves'; the copula is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the phrase asserts that the rulers are that burning grief. Note the ezāfe links are omitted in verse.
segments.s447-g9: بترس is an imperative (2nd-person singular) meaning “fear/be afraid (of...)”. The preposition ز (poetic for از) governs یزدان and means “of/from,” so ز یزدان بترس = “fear God.” The phrase ز ما شرم‌دار is adjectival with an implied verb (باش): “be ashamed of us” (شرم‌دار here = ‘ashamed’), coordinated with the first imperative by و (“and”).
segments.s447-g10: نگه کن is an imperative phrase: نگه (a noun/gerundal notion of ‘keeping/heed/watch’) + کن (do!), so “pay heed” / “mind.” بدین is the prepositional combination (به + این) meaning “by/with this” or “to this,” introducing the phrase گردش روزگار, an idiom meaning the turn or rotation of fate. روزگار here carries the sense of ‘fate/times.’
segments.s447-g11: یکی را is a direct-object phrase with the definite/accusative marker را (“one (person)” as the object). برآرد is a classical-present/epistolary third-person singular verb meaning “lifts up/raises” or “brings up.” The destination is marked by به + ابر بلند (“to a high/lofty cloud”), where بلند is a postpositive adjective modifying ابر.
segments.s447-g12: This clause contrasts with the previous one: یکی = “another/one (person).” ز (poetic از) before a pronoun (زو = ز او) means “from him/because of him.” شود is third-person singular present “becomes.” The sequence زار و خوار و نژند are coordinated adjectives (connected by و) describing the resulting state: ‘wretched, despised, base.’
segments.s447-g13: پدرت is پدر + the 2nd-person possessive suffix ت, “your father.” آن جهانگیر is an appositive epithet “that/world-conquering,” and لشکر فروز is a noun (لشکر, ‘host/army’) followed by the epithet فروز (postpositive adjective meaning ‘illustrious/glorious’), all stacked as descriptive titles for the father.
segments.s447-g14: نه negates the clause that follows. تابوت را is a definite object (the coffin). شد here functions as a (classical) verb meaning ‘was sent/moved’ or more literally ‘became (toward)’, and سوی نیمروز means ‘toward Nimruz’ (Nimruz can mean ‘midday’ or the southerly direction/region). The construction reads roughly as a passive/intransitive motion: ‘the coffin was not sent toward Nimruz.’
segments.s447-g15: نه at the start continues the negation from the previous clause (“nor”). رستم is the subject. The sequence به کابل به نخچیرگاه uses repeated به to mark movement/destination and the specific location: “to Kabul, to the hunting-ground.” This is a simple prepositional chain indicating where he did not go.
segments.s447-g16: بدان شد is a classical impersonal phrasing meaning roughly “it became known/it was so reported”: دان (root ‘to know’) plus شد (‘became’) produces a passive/impersonal sense. که introduces a subordinate clause. تا here has an archaic/subordinating sense (“otherwise/so that/that…”) and combines with نیست گردد + به چاه to express the danger: گردد (become/come to pass) with به چاه “into a pit.” The clause therefore explains the reason: it was known that otherwise he would be cast into a pit.
segments.s447-g41: تن کشته را is the definite direct object (را marks 'the slain body'); کردند is past plural 'they made/did'. دخمه ('tomb') and جای ('place') form the object complement: literally 'made a tomb‑place' = 'made a place in the tomb' (word order in classical/poetic Persian can place the object before the verb and put the complement after the verb).
segments.s447-g42: به here means 'by/according to' (instrumental or 'in accordance with'). گفتار دستور is a genitive/attributive chain ('the speech/word of the command' → 'the command/word'), and پاکیزه‌رای is an epithet modifying دستور ('of the pure‑minded' or 'pure‑of‑counsel'). The whole phrase qualifies the manner/authority for the action.
segments.s447-g43: ز (poetic 'from') زندان is the source and به ایوان the destination; گذر is the verbal noun 'passage/passing' and کرد is the past tense auxiliary 'did/made' forming the verb 'passed'. The proper name زال appears after the verb, a common poetic order (verb + subject).
segments.s447-g44: بگریست is past 'he/wept'. فرخ همال is the subject phrase 'Farrokh, the porter' placed after the verb. برو زار functions as an adverbial/qualifier meaning 'in great distress / mournfully' (an archaic poetic phrasing where برو intensifies or combines with زار 'mournful/wretched' to modify the verb).
segments.s447-g45: که introduces the reported/continuous content ('that' / 'when'). زارا and دلیرا here are vocative or exclamatory adjectives ('wretched, brave') in an archaic/poetic form (‑a ending); گوا immediately before رستما links as an epithet/marker ('witness' or 'thus/so') or functions to introduce the proper name رستما (Rostam) in the exclamation. رستما is the poetic/oblique form of the name Rostam used in classical verse.
segments.s447-g46: نبیره is 'offspring/scion'; گو here is the participial/attributive use of 'say' (―گو) meaning 'called/named' or '—speaking', so نبیره گو = 'called (a) scion'. نامور (preceding the name) is the adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying the following proper name نیرما, which is presented as the name applied. The clause therefore reports that someone was called 'the famed scion Nirma.'
segments.s447-g47: تو 'you' + تا زنده‌بودی 'until/while you were alive' — زنده‌بودی is a fused past second‑person form 'you were alive'. که introduces the content clause that follows; آگاه بود is past 'was aware/knew' (آگاه 'aware' + بود as past auxiliary). The whole means 'you, during your life, were aware that…'.
segments.s447-g48: که again introduces the content clause 'that…'. گشتاسپ is the personal name (Gushtasp). اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = in/within, جهان = world). شاه بود is the predicate 'was king' (شاه noun + بود past 'was').

sentences.s447-s9: So long as you live, O pure and generous king, do not vex anyone who has noble lineage.
sentences.s447-s10: When Nariman, the son of Sam, laments from his bonds to the lofty Lord.
sentences.s447-s11: Even if one turns aside from that — though fortunate by a good star — when the Creator casts down judgment.
sentences.s447-s12: When Rostam, guardian of the Kayanian throne, would bar the gate amid hardship.
sentences.s447-s29: He said to Bahman thus: "O new king, when the new moon is on the mid‑heaven..."
sentences.s447-s30: At nightfall send the army from this frontier, for this task has become difficult and burdensome.
sentences.s447-s31: May the eye be distant from your crown; may all your days be a feast.
sentences.s447-s32: In this house, Zal and Sam the brave — if it befit, the emperor will not remain long.
sentences.s447-s1: The noble Peshotan, who was a commander, was deeply grieved in his heart by the killing.
sentences.s447-s2: He rose before the sovereign, and thus said: 'O Khosrow, just and upright,'
sentences.s447-s3: If a grudge had been in your heart and you had wished it, it would have sprung from a lack of righteousness.
sentences.s447-s4: Now (carry out) plunder and killing and war and tumult; do it, and do not tolerate many outcries.
sentences.s447-s33: When a mountain rose upon the cheek of sandalwood, from the portal there rose the sound of the war-drum.
sentences.s447-s34: Then he commanded Bahman the Avenger to drive the whole host from there.
sentences.s447-s35: At that instant there issued from the curtain‑house the blast of horns and Indian instruments.
sentences.s447-s36: From there they set their faces toward Iran, at the command (word) of the magnanimous one.
sentences.s447-s17: When the king heard Peshotan's words, he became remorseful because of old pain and enmity.
sentences.s447-s18: A clamor arose from the curtain-chamber: 'O champions, with justice and counsel...'
sentences.s447-s19: Assemble many and return; beware lest you plunder and kill.
sentences.s447-s20: He commanded that the fetters be loosened from their feet and hands; they were loosened, and they gave much counsel.
sentences.s447-s13: You obtained this crown from him as a keepsake — not by the way (or right) of Gushtasp and Esfandiyar.
sentences.s447-s14: From the moment when Qobad came in thus, until Kay Khosrow the pure‑reputed.
sentences.s447-s15: They possessed greatness by his sword; all the guests were subject to him.
sentences.s447-s16: If by wisdom you can remove his bond, turn your heart back from the evil way.
sentences.s447-s25: Now the treasure was plundered and the hands taken captive; the wretched son was slain by an arrow.
sentences.s447-s26: May no eye behold this age; may the earth be without Esfandiyar’s seed.
sentences.s447-s27: On that news the report reached Bahman; it reached near the fortunate Peshotan.
sentences.s447-s28: Peshotan became sorrowful because of Rudabeh; from that wailing his steed grew pale.
sentences.s447-s37: He led the army from Zabul into Iran and brought it near the city of the brave.
sentences.s447-s38: He rested and sat upon the throne, joyful; he governed the world with custom and justice.
sentences.s447-s39: He gave a dervish some dirhams; of this, some were joyful and some sullen.
sentences.s447-s40: O world, what do you want of the rulers? The rulers are the burning grief of the hearts of slaves.
sentences.s447-s5: Fear God and be ashamed of us; heed this turning of the world's fortunes.
sentences.s447-s6: He sometimes raises one to a lofty cloud; another by him becomes wretched, despised, and base.
sentences.s447-s7: Your father — that world-conqueror, illustrious commander of the host — did not have his coffin sent toward Nimruz (the south/noon).
sentences.s447-s8: Nor did Rostam go to Kabul’s hunting-ground, for it became known that otherwise he would be cast into a pit.
sentences.s447-s21: They made a place in the tomb for the slain body, according to the command/word of the pure‑minded.
sentences.s447-s22: Zal passed from the prison to the porch. Farrokh the porter wept bitterly.
sentences.s447-s23: They cried aloud, calling out (in lament): 'O wretched one, O brave one — Rostam!' and they named him the famed scion Nirma.
sentences.s447-s24: You, while you were alive, knew that Gushtasp was king in the world.
